---
title: "The Disagreement Has a Shape"
subtitle: "Part 2 of a series on the meta-NPS. Run five expert systems across the whole carbohydrate supply and most foods come back agreed. The contested ones aren't scattered at random — they pile up in one aisle, and they're worst exactly where America eats the most."
author:
  - "Josh Erndt-Marino, PhD"
  - "FRESH's AI assistant"
date: "2026-06-25"
description: |
  Part 1 showed one loaf earning five expert verdicts. Part 2 runs that
  meta-rating over every carb-rich food to ask how often the experts split, and
  where. The answer has a shape: about a fifth of carb foods are
  contested, grains most of all, and how much we eat a food decides which of
  those splits to settle first.
categories: [meta-NPS, carbohydrate-quality, IAFNS, FRESH, interactive]
fresh-engines: [Meta-NPS, Healthfulness Map]
topics: [Carb Quality]
series: "Meta-NPS"
part: 2
card-subtitle: "Run five expert systems across the whole carbohydrate supply and most foods agree. The contested ones pile up in one aisle, worst where America eats most."
resources:
  - categories.html
  - fingerprints.html
  - map.html
  - foursplit.html
---

In [Part 1](../meta-nps-five-verdicts/), one loaf of whole-wheat bread earned
five different expert verdicts, from the 12th percentile to the 99th. The
meta-NPS turned that spread into two numbers a person can actually read: a
**meta-score** for how healthy the experts call it on balance, and a
**consensus** measure for how much they agree.

One food is an anecdote. The obvious next question is the one the second webinar
set out to answer: run the same five systems across the *entire* carbohydrate
supply — roughly 6,500 foods — and how often do they split like that? And when
they do, is it random, or does the disagreement have a shape?

## Four foods, four verdicts

Start with the two numbers together. Meta-score puts a food high or low.
Consensus says whether the experts meant it. Cross them and you get four
honest categories, not one tidy score: **certainly healthy**, **certainly
unhealthy**, **certainly neutral** (they agree it's middling), and the category
the meta-NPS exists to surface — **uncertain** (they don't agree at all).

Those four categories aren't abstract. Watch four foods sort into them.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="catFrame" src="categories.html?v=1" title="Interactive: four foods sorting into four healthfulness categories" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:560px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
<!-- BUILD SPEC — Reveal A (faithful to webinar 2 slides 11-13, cross-slide build) -->
<!-- Engine: reuse meta-nps-five-verdicts/boxplot.html (responsive, height-aware). -->
<!-- y = healthfulness percentile; one dot per expert system; box = consensus (IQR). -->
<!-- Beat 1 (~5s): apple lands top + tight (certainly healthy), croissant lands bottom + tight (certainly unhealthy) — the two clear poles. -->
<!-- Beat 2 (~5s): potato chips lands mid + tight — certainly neutral; they agree it's middling. -->
<!-- Beat 3 (~6s): white rice lands mid but split wide (one system ~25th, others 60th-95th) — uncertain. Caption: "the surprise — and we'll come back to foods like it." -->
<!-- Data: apple + white rice already public in tool-healthfulness-map/story.html; potato chips + croissant rendered from rounded values consistent with the public webinar. -->
```

Two of those four are easy. Apple sits near the top, the systems bunched tight:
certainly healthy. Croissant sits at the bottom, just as tightly agreed:
certainly unhealthy. Potato chips is the third, and it lands in the middle with
the systems *still* bunched, so they agree it's a so-so food. That's a real
verdict. The fourth only looks like it. White rice lands in the middle too, but
the systems fan from the bottom quarter to the top: some treat it almost like a
vegetable, others almost like a sweet. "Middle" was hiding two opposite things.
One is a verdict. The other is a shrug.

## Most foods aren't the argument

Here's the part that gets lost when people say experts "can't agree on
anything." Across all carb-rich foods, they mostly do. Only about a fifth come
back uncertain. The disagreement is the exception, not the rule.

But that fifth isn't spread evenly. Sort the supply into food groups and each
one has its own fingerprint.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="fpFrame" src="fingerprints.html?v=2" title="Interactive: each food group's mix of healthfulness categories, grains most uncertain" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:560px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
<!-- BUILD SPEC — Reveal B (faithful to slides 14-17; pie-per-group fingerprints, A/B/C/D table on desktop, pies+hover on mobile) -->
<!-- b1 headline (s14): two summary pies, blue ring on the uncertain wedge — All carb ~20% vs Grains ~50%. -->
<!-- b2 (s15): cross-fade to the table; F&V pie -> example foods settle into columns; pulse F&V (only healthy/neutral). pie=whole group, cells=samples. -->
<!-- b3 (s16): Desserts settles; honey pulses in the Uncertain column (the common-food surprise). -->
<!-- b4 (s16): grains/beverages/mixed settle — every group has its own fingerprint. -->
<!-- b5 (s17): the blue box draws on around the Uncertain column; rank badges 1/2/3 on grains/mixed/beverages (50/33/27%). -->
<!-- b6 (s17): gray-area gather — dim all but the Uncertain column + blue wedges; bridge to Reveal C. -->
<!-- empty cells are faithful to the slide (F&V no uncertain/unhealthy; desserts no healthy/neutral; mixed no healthy). -->
```

Fruits and vegetables show the cleanest fingerprint: almost entirely agreed, and
agreed *healthy*, with no certainly-unhealthy foods at all. Desserts flip the
other way, mostly unhealthy, though even there a commonly eaten food like honey
hides in the uncertain slice. And grains are the gray zone, with roughly half of
them contested, more than any other group, followed by mixed dishes and
beverages.

Each group's slice mix is its full composition across the supply; the individual
foods named in the walkthrough are a recognizable sample, drawn in proportion to
that mix from the 122 most-commonly-eaten carbohydrate foods. The cells
illustrate the fingerprint — they are not the whole list.

So the disagreement has a shape after all. It concentrates, and grains are the
epicenter. The bread from Part 1 wasn't a fluke. It was a postcard from the most
uncertain aisle in the store.

## Which disagreements are worth resolving

A contested food the experts can't settle barely matters if no one eats it. The
lever, as in Part 1, is **consumption**. The foods worth a panel's time are the
ones that are uncertain *and* widely eaten, because that's where harmonizing the
science would actually move diets.

This is the tool doing exactly that. The map plots every food by healthfulness
(up the screen) and by how much America eats it (across). The 122 foods shown
account for about half of all US carbohydrate intake. The walkthrough does what
we did in the webinar: filter to the contested middle, shift right to the
most-eaten, then order by disagreement until four foods are left.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="mapFrame" src="map.html?v=1" title="Interactive: filtering the healthfulness-impact map to the most uncertain, most-eaten carb foods" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:600px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
<!-- BUILD SPEC — Reveal C (faithful to slides 19-22; extend the responsive map in tool-healthfulness-map/story.html) -->
<!-- Beat 1 (~5s): the full map, 122 foods, healthfulness x consumption. "122 foods = ~50% of US carb intake." -->
<!-- Beat 2 (~6s): draw the selection rectangle around the uncertain/neutral middle, shifted RIGHT (more-consumed). -->
<!-- Beat 3 (~6s): collapse to the 17 inside the rectangle; order by rank range (wider range = more disagreement). "122 -> 17." -->
<!-- Beat 4 (~6s): narrow to the top 4 by rank range: wheat bread, sweetened iced tea, white rice, white bread. "the most uncertain AND impactful." -->
```

Filter to the foods the experts call uncertain. Shift right, toward the ones
America actually eats. Then order what's left by how far apart the systems land.
A hundred and twenty-two foods become seventeen, and seventeen become four:
**wheat bread, sweetened iced tea, white rice, and white bread.** The most
contested, most-eaten carbohydrate foods in the American diet.

## Four foods, no easy answer

Pull those four back onto the boxplot and the problem is stark. Every one of
them has a rank range above 69 — the systems disagree so widely that each food
is essentially *healthy or unhealthy depending on who you ask.* Not a rounding
error between the 60th and 70th percentile. The full reach of the scale, four
times over, on foods tens of millions of people eat every day.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="fourFrame" src="foursplit.html?v=1" title="Interactive: the four most contested foods, each with expert scores spanning the full scale" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:560px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
<!-- BUILD SPEC — Reveal D (faithful to slides 23-25; mirrors Reveal A's boxplot engine) -->
<!-- Beat 1 (s23): the four boxplots appear; dots = the 5 systems, box = IQR, line = meta. -->
<!-- Beat 2 (s24): gold rank-range glyph (min->max) grows beside each box; values 79/72/85/69; all > 69. -->
<!-- Beat 3 (s25): system labels overlay; pose "does any one system rank these four the same way?" -->
<!-- Beat 4 (de-novo): trace FPro across the four (on-demand "spaghetti") — high for rice/tea, low for breads = processing cuts opposite ways, no shared lever. Resting state: hover any system to trace it. Part-1 (whole-wheat) handoff lives in the prose. -->
```

So we went looking for what drives these splits — some factor that could explain
the disagreement across all four. The likeliest suspect is processing: the two
systems that score it sink both breads. But it isn't a clean driver. The same
systems rank white rice and iced tea near the top, so processing cuts opposite
ways across the foods instead of explaining them. One loaf did resolve that way back in Part
1: [setting aside the processing
systems](../meta-nps-five-verdicts/#the-fight-was-about-processing) collapsed the
disagreement for whole-wheat bread, a different loaf from the wheat bread here,
and settled its verdict to healthy. That is a finding about *where* the experts
are arguing, which is the first thing you would need before anyone could resolve
it.

## Where this goes

That's the contribution the meta-NPS is reaching for. Not a better single score,
but an honest map of the gray area: how much of the supply is contested, where it
clusters, which contested foods are worth resolving first, and a way to test what
drives each disagreement. Surfacing the gap is the first step toward closing it.

Two honest limits, the same ones the webinar ended on. Harmonizing this universal
disagreement is the floor, not the ceiling. Every system here, and the meta-NPS
on top of them, rates one food the same way for everyone, and "healthy for whom"
is a question none of them ask. The floor is harmonization; the ceiling is
personalization, and both are buildable now. And defining healthfulness is as
much art as science — there is no proof of the single best system, so the goal
isn't to crown one. It's to show, food by food, how settled the science actually
is.

There's one more thing the experts' map can't tell you: whether anyone *outside*
the panels sees food the same way. Part 3 brings in the public, and what happens
when expert verdicts and everyday belief pull apart.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The categories, the food-group shares (about 20% of carb foods contested, near
50% of grains), the 122 foods that make up roughly half of US carbohydrate
intake, and the four most contested staples are all from the public IAFNS
*Insights From the Tool* webinar. The numbers are a conservative floor: only
five of hundreds of published rating systems were sampled, and a single
consensus cutoff drew the lines — a different defensible cutoff flags more, not
fewer. The webinars and papers behind the series are on the
[resources page](../resources/).
:::

```{=html}
<script>
(function(){
  var frames=document.querySelectorAll("iframe.story-frame");
  function fit(f){ try{ var d=f.contentDocument; if(d&&d.body){ f.style.height=(d.body.scrollHeight+8)+"px"; } }catch(e){} }
  function sendVp(f){ try{ f.contentWindow.postMessage({kind:"vph",h:window.innerHeight},"*"); }catch(e){} }
  frames.forEach(function(f){
    f.addEventListener("load",function(){ sendVp(f); fit(f); setTimeout(function(){ sendVp(f); fit(f); },400); });
  });
  window.addEventListener("resize",function(){ frames.forEach(function(f){ sendVp(f); fit(f); }); });
  window.addEventListener("message",function(e){ if(e&&e.data==="story-fit"){ frames.forEach(fit); } });
  if("IntersectionObserver" in window){
    var io=new IntersectionObserver(function(es){
      es.forEach(function(en){ if(en.isIntersecting){ try{ en.target.contentWindow.postMessage("play-story","*"); }catch(e){} } });
    }, {threshold:0.25});
    frames.forEach(function(f){ io.observe(f); });
  }
})();
</script>
```
